coredata.dc:title: Neglected negative effect of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) entering the ocean on marine organisms living in different water layers
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xinyang
Chen, Ciara Chun
Wu, Lin
Zhou, Jin
Huang, Yuxiong
Zhu, Xiaoshan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are well dispersed in water, but their potential risks in the marine environment have not been described. This study characterized CQDs and investigated their biological effects (including growth, photosynthesis and behavioural changes) in three marine organisms living in different water layers (the surface phytoplankton Phaeodactylum tricornutum and zooplankton Artemia salina and the benthic coral Zoanthus sp. at the bottom). The results showed that over 78 % of CQDs were suspended in seawater after 96 h. The biomass and photosynthesis of P. tricornutum were significantly affected, with a maximum reduction of 89.49 % in algal cells. CQDs accumulated in the intestinal tract of A. salina, reducing grazing and filtration rates by up to 71.88 % and 89.46 %, respectively. In contrast, CQD exposure had irreversible effects on the tentacle expansion behaviour of Zoanthus sp. This study helps clarify the environmental effects and ecological risks associated with the release of CQDs into the ocean.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Environmental effect
Toxic effect
Influence mechanism
Ecological risk
Marine environment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of test organisms and CQD suspensions
Exposure of the phytoplankton P. tricornutum to CQDs
Toxicity and feeding behaviour of the zooplankton A. salina under CQD exposure
Effects of CQD settling on the photosynthetic coral Zoanthus sp.
Effects of CQDs on growth and photosynthesis in P. tricornutum
Toxic effects of CQDs on A. salina
Toxic effect of CQDs on Zoanthus sp.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Relative content of CQDs in (a) A. salina and Zoanthus sp. medium (artificial seawater) and (b) P. tricornutum medium (f/2 medium).
Effects of CQDs on the content of (a) biomass (b) Fv/Fm and (c) Chlorophyll-a in P. tricornutum.
The grazing and filtration rates of A. salina fed with P. tricornutum after (a) 12 h and (b) 24 h. Different capital or lowercase letters indicated a significant difference among the treatments.
(a) The full expansion rate of Zoanthus sp. tentacles in different periods under CQDs exposure; (b) Effect of different concentrations of CQDs on coral tentacles after 24 h exposure.

head.ce:title: Neglected negative effect of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) entering the ocean on marine organisms living in different water layers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen International Graduate School, Tsinghua University, Shenzhen 518055, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shenzhen International Graduate School, Tsinghua University, Shenzhen 518055, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Ecology and Environment, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: X. Zhu, School of Ecology and Environment, Hainan University, Haikou 570208, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are well dispersed in water, but their potential risks in the marine environment have not been described. This study characterized CQDs and investigated their biological effects (including growth, photosynthesis and behavioural changes) in three marine organisms living in different water layers (the surface phytoplankton Phaeodactylum tricornutum and zooplankton Artemia salina and the benthic coral Zoanthus sp. at the bottom). The results showed that over 78 % of CQDs were suspended in seawater after 96 h. The biomass and photosynthesis of P. tricornutum were significantly affected, with a maximum reduction of 89.49 % in algal cells. CQDs accumulated in the intestinal tract of A. salina, reducing grazing and filtration rates by up to 71.88 % and 89.46 %, respectively. In contrast, CQD exposure had irreversible effects on the tentacle expansion behaviour of Zoanthus sp. This study helps clarify the environmental effects and ecological risks associated with the release of CQDs into the ocean.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development and extensive use of nanotechnology, the environmental risks of manufactured nanomaterials (MNMs) have attracted increasing public and regulatory concern (Miller and Poland, 2020). As a new frontier for MNMs, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), spherical or sphere-like MNMs <10 nm in size (Silva and Gonçalves, 2011; Abd Rani et al., 2021; Tay et al., 2022), have been widely used in biomarkers, medical imaging, optoelectronics and other related fields in recent years due to their good biocompatibility and fluorescence stability (Abd Rani et al., 2021; Kumar et al., 2022). Additionally, various oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface lead to their good dispersibility and persistence in aqueous solutions (Liu et al., 2009; Achraf et al., 2011). This means that CQDs could migrate over long distances with water flow after entering the aquatic environment (Zheng et al., 2017). However, the effects of the widespread distribution of these MNMs in real aquatic environments are still unknown.

A series of studies on the biosafety and ecological impact of CQDs have been carried out in freshwater, and the results suggest negative effects on the growth and physiological process of organisms. For instance, after 144 h of exposure to 500 mg/L PEG2000-CQDs, the abundance of Microcystis aeruginosa decreased to only 1.35 % of that in the control (Yan et al., 2015). With increasing dose and exposure time of CQDs, photosynthesis of Scenedesmus obliquus was gradually inhibited, and chlorophyll a decreased by 94.9 % after 96 h of exposure to 500 mg/L CQDs (Yao et al., 2018). For higher trophic levels, CQDs accumulated in the gut and liver of Cyprinus carpio and caused oxidative stress after 5 weeks of oral exposure (Chen et al., 2022). In addition, the food chain delivery and nutrient transfer of CQDs between Chlorella pyrenoidosa and Daphnia magna have been confirmed (Liu et al., 2022). However, limited studies have been conducted on the biological and ecological effects of CQDs in marine environments.

Compared to freshwater, the marine environment is a more complex system containing a large number of inorganic colloids and natural organic matter with higher ionic strength and salinity (Lowry et al., 2012; Made et al., 2017). This would influence the stability of CQDs in seawater, resulting in similar behaviours, such as aggregation, agglomeration, and sedimentation, to those of other MNMs (Corsi et al., 2020; Piresa et al., 2022). Given the inevitable release of CQDs into the marine environment due to their increasing use (Baker et al., 2014), marine ecosystems could be their ultimate sink (Klaine et al., 2008). Thus, it is important to confirm this potential large system of CQDs and clarify their distribution and impact on marine ecosystems.

To better understand the fate and consequences of CQDs in marine environments, the biological effects of organisms living in different water layers were investigated in this study. The impacts of CQDs on the growth and photosynthesis of the phytoplankton Phaeodactylum tricornutum, a ubiquitous primary producer living in surface seawater, were determined. The bioaccumulation and toxicity of CQDs in the zooplankton Artemia salina, which feeds on P. tricornutum and is distributed in the surface layers of seawater, were studied. In addition, the immobilization under CQD exposure of the photosynthetic coral Zoanthus sp., which is not only a susceptible benthic organism but also a common species of the most biologically and economically productive ecosystems—coral reefs—was tested. This study, for the first time, attempts to illustrate the marine environmental impact of CQDs via their biological effects on various marine organisms from different water layers. This work provides new insight for understanding the environmental behaviours of CQDs after their release and objectively assessing the environmental risks of CQDs in marine environments.

CQDs were made by a hydrothermal method as previously reported with an average particle size of 2–3 nm (Fig. S1) (Zhu et al., 2013; Yao et al., 2018). Briefly, 4.20 g of citric acid powder was weighed and poured into 40 mL of ultrapure water to form a citric acid solution, and then 1.34 mL of ethylenediamine solution was measured and added into the citric acid solution; after ultrasonication and mixing, the mixture was transferred into an autoclave with a polytetrafluoroethylene liner and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After the end of the heating process, it was cooled naturally to room temperature, and then the reaction material was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant after centrifugation was dialyzed with a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000. During the dialysis period, the ultrapure water was replaced every 4 h according to the colour change of the solution outside the dialysis bag; a total of 24 h of dialysis was carried out. The obtained solution was vacuum freeze-dried to obtain yellowish-brown CQD solids.

The morphology and aggregation of CQDs in ultrapure water and artificial seawater were observed by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM; Tecnai™ G2, FEI, USA) The surface functional groups of the CQDs were determined by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR; Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS 50, USA).

The absorption peak of the CQDs used in this experiment was determined to be 340 nm using a microplate reader (Infinite M200 Pro, Tecan, Switzerland) (Fig. S2). Subsequently, the microplate reader was also used to measure the absorbance of the supernatant at 340 nm to detect the precipitation of CQDs in the culture medium. The luminescence properties and fluorescence stability of the CQDs were determined by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F97, Leng'guang, China) at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of test organisms and CQD suspensions
ce:section.ce:para: A. salina and Zoanthus sp. were acclimatized in artificial seawater (salinity = 30 ± 1.91 PSU, pH = 8.23 ± 0.05), while P. tricornutum was cultured using f/2 medium (Guillard and Ryther, 1962). The artificial seawater used in this study was fully aerated for 24 h before use.

Five hundred milligrams of CQDs were weighed and dissolved in 500 mL of artificial seawater and f/2 medium to prepare a 1 g/L suspension of CQDs, which was stored in reagent bottles away from light. The CQD suspension was ultrasonically dissolved for 30 min before use to ensure uniform dispersion of the particles. After sonication, the CQD suspensions were diluted to the desired concentrations for the exposure experiments with the three species detailed in the following sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exposure of the phytoplankton P. tricornutum to CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: P. tricornutum was obtained from Guang'yu Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and cultured in autoclaved 150 mL flasks filled with 100 mL of f/2 medium (containing silica). All of the flasks were placed in an illumination incubator (GZH-128 A, Huier Corp., China), and the algae were cultured at 20 °C under cool white fluorescent light (12 h light:12 h dark) at a light intensity of 3000 lx. These flasks were shaken three times per day (at 8 h intervals) to keep the algae under good growth conditions (Wang et al., 2016). The algae were cultured to the exponential phase before transfer to new culture medium for the following tests.

Seven control groups with different CQD concentrations (0, 5, 10, 50, 100, 200, 500 mg/L) were set up to determine the impact of CQDs on the growth of P. tricornutum (4 × 104 cells/mL). A haemocytometer was used to record the number of algae per day under an optical microscope (Nikon, Japan). The growth inhibition rates of algae at different concentrations were calculated according to the OECD standard (OECD, 1984; Supporting Information). The maximal quantum yields of photosystem II (PSII) photochemistry (Fv/fm; dual modulation fluorometer, FL3500, PSI, Czech Republic) and chlorophyll a (phytoplankton fluorometer, Ci-L, Nikon, Japan) were calculated to describe the impact on the photosynthetic capacity of P. tricornutum. The encapsulation effect of CQDs on P. tricornutum was demonstrated by fluorescence microscopy (DS-U3, Nikon, Japan), and the stability of CQDs and algae after mixing was characterized using a zeta potential meter (Omni, NanoBrook, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity and feeding behaviour of the zooplankton A. salina under CQD exposure
ce:section.ce:para: A. salina eggs were purchased from Haiding Aquatic Products Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China) and stored in the dark at 4°C until use. The eggs were incubated in a hatching funnel for 24 h at 24 ± 1 °C under a light source (3000–4000 lx).

The toxicity of CQDs to the zooplankton A. salina was assessed according to OECD guideline 202 (OECD, 1984). The survival rates of A. salina at different life stages were calculated under various CQD concentrations of 0, 5, 10, 50, 100, 200, and 500 mg/L (the same below) after 72 h. Ten A. salina nauplii were added to 2 mL CQD solution in 24-well plates for each nauplius life stage and each CQD concentration. The number of dead A. salina larvae (motionless for 10 s) was recorded every 24 h under a stereomicroscope.

To investigate the effects of CQDs on the feeding behaviour of A. salina, P. tricornutum was used as a food source, and the filtration rate and grazing rate of A. salina were calculated. Twenty nauplii of instar I were added to each 10 mL test tube under different concentrations of CQDs for a 24 h adaptation (no feeding during this period). Then, 5 viable nauplii of instar II were selected from each concentration and added to a well plate containing 1 mL CQDs at the corresponding concentration. P. tricornutum at the exponential phase was added to each well of the plate to fix the volume to 2 mL (4 × 104 cells/mL). The well plates were shaken every 8 h to prevent the algal solution from settling. After 24 h, the filtration rate and grazing rate of A. salina were calculated using Frost's method (Frost, 1972; Supporting Information), and the effect of CQDs on artemia feeding was observed using fluorescence microscopy. The life stages of A. salina in the feeding test were categorized into the instar I nauplius stage (< 24h, the day nauplii are removed from the hatching funnel), instar II nauplius stage (24–48h), and instar III nauplius stage (48–72 h) (Pillard and Tapp, 2021). For the feeding experiment, the glass well plates were placed in a rack and maintained at 24 ± 1 °C under dark conditions to avoid algal growth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CQD settling on the photosynthetic coral Zoanthus sp.
ce:section.ce:para: Zoanthus sp. was purchased from Xing'guo Marine Farm (Zhangzhou, China). According to our preliminary observations, the tentacles of Zoanthus sp. usually extend during the day and shrink in dark conditions. In this study, two periods (acute exposure and recovery) were set up to systematically understand the response of Zoanthus sp. under CQD exposure. The degrees of inhibition of Zoanthus sp. based on the extension of coral tentacles were defined as fully expanded, partially expanded and closed. The growth inhibition rate of Zoanthus sp. was determined by counting the individuals with tentacles fully expanded or closed in the daytime every 24 h. Acute exposure to CQDs was conducted for 72 h under concentrations of 0, 1, 5, 10, 50, and 100 mg/L to test the sensitivity of the corals. The corals were transferred to the growth environment (clean artificial seawater) after acute exposure for recovery, and the time was extended to 192 h.

One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed by SPSS 23.0 (IBM Corporation, Armonk, NY) software, and comparisons between groups were performed by Dunnett's t-test. All experiments were performed in triplicate, and the results are presented as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). Differences were considered statistically significant when p < 0.05.

The TEM results showed that the CQDs were spherical nanoparticles with good dispersibility in the three media (Fig. S3), which were similar to the properties of materials applied in freshwater studies (Yao et al., 2018; Ahlawat et al., 2021; Gohari et al., 2021). However, the particle sizes were different, which could be attributed to variations in the behaviour of MNMs in aqueous environments due to the nature of the nanoparticles and the environmental conditions (Turan et al., 2019). Generally, an increase in salinity or ionic strength can reduce the negativity of the electrophoretic mobility of nanoparticles to promote their aggregation (Batley et al., 2013). CQD agglomerates were observed in artificial seawater (6–15 nm; Fig. S3 b, c), indicating that CQDs could become larger after entering the ocean, which would lead to aggregation or sedimentation and thus may cause further impacts on the growth and survival of planktonic and benthic organisms. In addition, the FT-IR spectra revealed absorption peaks of CQDs at 3336.81 cm−1 and 1720 cm−1 that could be ascribed to the stretching vibrations of OH and CO (Fig. S4). The absorption peaks at 2944.41 cm−1 and 1437.1 cm−1 were due to the stretching and bending vibrations of CH, and the absorption peaks at 1534.41 cm−1 and 1376.86 cm−1 were attributed to the bending vibrations of NH and C-NH-C (Fig. S4). This suggested that the surface of the prepared CQDs contained various functional groups, including hydroxyl groups, carboxyl groups and nitrogen-containing groups (Yang et al., 2017). The abundance of these hydrophilic groups on the surface of CQDs further explains their high water solubility (Zhu et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2022).

For the fluorescence performance, under an excitation wavelength of 340 nm, the fluorescence intensities of the CQDs in the three media showed a trend of first increasing and then decreasing (Fig. S5 a,b and c). The maximum emission wavelength was 450 nm, which belongs to the wavelength range of blue light. The fluorescence intensities of CQDs in different media increased with concentration (Fig. S5 a, b and c). Consistent with other studies, the CQDs typically showed significant optical absorption in the UV region with tails extending into the visible range (Baker and Baker, 2010; Li et al., 2012). It was also found that the nature of the media had limited effects on the fluorescence performance of the CQDs (Fig. S5d). Such unique optical properties contribute to the wide application of CQDs (Song et al., 2020).

Most studies have shown that MNMs tend to aggregate and sink to the bottom in a short time after their release into the marine environment (Sun et al., 2013; Sendra et al., 2017; Lv et al., 2020). However, this is not the case for CQDs. In our natural settling experiment, the relative content of CQDs in the supernatant at all concentrations was found to exceed 78 % after 96 h (Fig. 1 ), while that of nTiO2 was only approximately 60 % after 2 h of settling (Wang et al., 2016). This indicated that CQDs would pose a higher threat to plankton. The results of wavelength scanning of the collected sediment demonstrated that the CQDs also produced a small amount of sedimentation over time (Fig. S6). The sediment at the highest concentration (500 mg/L) was only weakly responsive, which also reflected its slower settling (Fig. S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CQDs on growth and photosynthesis in P. tricornutum
ce:section.ce:para: The growth inhibition rates of P. tricornutum exposed to various CQD concentrations (Fig. 2a) showed that there were no obvious inhibitory effects on the growth of P. tricornutum under low CQD concentrations (≤ 10 mg/L). Moreover, there was even a certain degree of “toxic excitation”, as indicated by the significant increase in biomass (Fig. 2a). This phenomenon has generally been taken as a hormesis effect (Morelli et al., 2013; Agathokleous et al., 2019), which was also observed in studies on quantum dots (QDs) (Xiao et al., 2012) and carbon dots (CDs) (Liu et al., 2022). It should also be noted that CQDs are carbon-based MNMs that can improve the growth of microalgae by increasing the light absorption spectrum (Giraldo et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2018). However, the growth inhibition of the algae by CQDs was gradually enhanced with increasing exposure concentration (> 10 mg/L). When the concentrations of CQDs were 100 and 200 mg/L, the abundance of algae cells decreased by 81.11 % and 89.49 %, respectively, compared to the control after 120 h, while the growth of P. tricornutum was completely inhibited under exposure to 500 mg/L CQDs (Fig. 2a). As the microalgal CQD exposure concentration increased, the hormonal response was slowly overcome by the harmful effects of high concentrations of CQDs, which was consistent with the results of other MNM exposure experiments (Schwab et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2020a; Lau et al., 2022).

The effects of CQDs on photosynthesis in P. tricornutum were further investigated. The variations in Fv/fm and chlorophyll a in P. tricornutum under different CQD concentrations are shown in Fig. 2b, c. Low concentrations of CQDs (≤10 mg/L) promoted Fv/fm and chlorophyll a, which are presumably related to the photocatalytic properties of CQDs (Jung et al., 2022). The photosynthetic efficiency of P. tricornutum was gradually suppressed with increasing CQD concentration. One possible explanation is that the nanomaterials agglomerate in the water, thus blocking part of the light (Fabienne et al., 2011; Skjolding et al., 2022). They may also be attached to the surface of algal cells, absorbing or blocking some of the light and thus affecting their photosynthesis (Lin et al., 2009).

To verify how CQDs affect the growth and photosynthesis of P. tricornutum, we observed the aggregation of CQDs with algae at the highest exposure concentration (500 mg/L) by fluorescence microscopy and found that the algae were wrapped by CQDs (Fig. S7); similar results were obtained in a study of nTiO2 and nano-ZnO (Wang et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2016). The zeta potentials of both CQDs and algae under gradient CQD concentrations also demonstrated the ease of agglomeration when the two components coexist (Fig. S8). This confirmed our inference that CQDs affect the photosynthesis of P. tricornutum by the shading effect caused by aggregation. Specifically, the shading effect would change the light, pigments and other conditions required for photosynthesis, weakening the absorption of light by algae and thus inhibiting the process of photosynthesis (Li et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2018; Todorenko et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxic effects of CQDs on A. salina
ce:section.ce:para: Bioaccumulation and trophic transfer of MNMs through the aquatic food chain may have toxic effects on higher trophic levels (Wang et al., 2019). In our study, algae encapsulated by CQDs (Fig. S7) had a significant effect on the feeding behaviour of the grazer A. salina. First, the optimal age for feeding experiments was determined by exploring the effects of exposure to CQDs on A. salina nauplii at different growth stages (Fig. S9). It was found that CQDs had a significant lethal effect on the instar I nauplius stage at only the highest exposure concentration (p < 0.05). The mortality was approximately 30.00 % at 48 h and 43.33 % at 72 h. Other exposure groups did not have significant effects over time. The results indirectly demonstrated the low toxicity and good biocompatibility of CQDs (Tungare et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020b; Chung et al., 2021).

Changes in the feeding behaviour of zooplankton may indirectly affect their normal growth and reproduction. The results of the 12 h and 24 h feeding experiments showed that the exposure concentration of CQDs had a significant effect on the grazing rate and filtration rate of A. salina (Fig. 3 , p < 0.05). Both rates gradually decreased as the exposure concentration of CQDs increased. The grazing rate and filtration rate of A. salina decreased by 71.88 % and 89.46 %, respectively, after exposure to the highest concentration (500 mg/L) of CQDs for 12 h (Fig. 3a). By further validation under fluorescence microscopy, we observed a significant accumulation of CQDs in the guts of A. salina compared to the control (Fig. 3a, b). In other words, CQDs could cause a blockage after entering the gut, directly affecting the feeding behaviour of the grazer. Predators that feed on low trophic levels containing MNMs can exhibit abnormal behaviour, and such cascading effects of short food chain transmission are common in the ecological impact of MNMs (Bouldin et al., 2008; Gilroy et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxic effect of CQDs on Zoanthus sp.
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in the results of our natural settling experiments, CQDs could sink in the same way as other MNMs, such as by adsorption and aggregation (Fig. 1d,e). The effects of CQDs after settling were further verified by the experiments with benthic Zoanthus sp. (Fig. 4 ). When Zoanthus sp. was exposed to relatively low concentrations of CQDs (≤ 5 mg/L), the expansion behaviour of its tentacles was not significantly affected. However, as the exposure concentration increased (≥ 10 mg/L), the expansion behaviour was gradually restricted (Fig. 4b). It was also shown that the addition of higher concentrations of CQDs (≥ 50 mg/L) caused immediate closure of the tentacles of Zoanthus sp. The coral tentacles were almost continuously and completely closed after 72 h of exposure to 100 mg/L CQDs, with no signs of recovery (Fig. 4a). The organisms were then immediately transferred to the previous culture environment (clean artificial seawater), but they did not fully recover under almost all concentration exposures, even after an extended period of 120 h (Fig. 4a). The results showed that the immobilization of Zoanthus sp. was positively correlated with the exposure concentration of CQDs, and such inhibition was difficult to overcome by prolonging the time (Fig. 4a). The impact of CQDs on benthic organisms cannot be ignored, especially in the current situation in which the living environment of corals has been gradually affected by MNMs (Marangoni et al., 2022).

With the increasing use of CQDs and their high water solubility, they will inevitably enter the aquatic environment and ultimately the oceans. To assess the ecological risk of CQDs in marine environments, the morphology and physicochemical properties of CQDs in seawater were investigated, and the effects of CQDs on marine organisms in different water layers after entering the ocean were studied for the first time. The results showed the following: (1) CQDs tended to have good dispersion in artificial seawater and formed agglomerates (6–15 nm) but were unlikely to produce significant sedimentation for at least 96 h; (2) exposure to higher CQD concentrations (> 10 mg/L) led to significant inhibition of the growth and photosynthetic process of the phytoplankton P. tricornutum, probably due to the shading effect on algae encapsulated by aggregates of CQDs; and (3) on the basis of the 12 h and 24 h feeding experiments, the grazing and filtration rates of the zooplankton A. salina decreased significantly with increasing CQD concentration, and varying degrees of intestinal CQD accumulation were found in A. salina nauplii exposed to 500 mg/L CQDs for two durations (12 h and 24 h). (4) The deposition of CQDs could lead to tentacle closure in the benthic organism Zoanthus sp., which could be irreversible. This study provides the basis for a thorough understanding of the fate of CQDs in the marine environment and is important for objectively assessing the marine ecological risks of CQDs. It should, however, be noted that the concentration of CQDs in real environments is lower than that used in the present study. Nevertheless, in some scenarios, such as sewage outfalls with potentially high concentrations of CQDs, in view of the long-term accumulation of CQDs in the environment, their negative effects on organisms should not be neglected.

Xinyang Li: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Ciara Chun Chen: Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Lin Wu: Formal analysis, Investigation. Jin Zhou: Formal analysis, Investigation. Yuxiong Huang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Xiaoshan Zhu: Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (42077227, 42006141), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2021A1515010158, 2023A1515011101) and Sustainable Development Special Project of Shenzhen Science and Technology Innovation Committee (KCXFZ20230731093402005).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpolbul.2023.115921.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Abd Rani et al., 2021

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photocatalytic degradation of crystal violet dye using Sulphur-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Abd Rani, U., Ng, L.Y., Ng, C.Y., Mahmoudi, E., Hairom, N.H.H., 2021. Photocatalytic degradation of crystal violet dye using sulphur-doped carbon quantum dots. Mater. Today Proc. 46, 1934-1939. doi:10.1016/j.matpr.2021.02.225.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Achraf et al., 2011

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In vivo biodistribution and biological impact of injected carbon nanotubes using magnetic resonance techniques

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Achraf, A.F., Florence, F., Nathalie, L., Ghislaine, L., Michael, L., Yannick, C., Emmanuelle C.S., 2011. In vivo biodistribution and biological impact of injected carbon nanotubes using magnetic resonance techniques. Int. J. Nanomed. 6, 351-361. doi:10.2147/IJN.S16653.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zheng, G.Q., Lv, X.H., Zhu, X.S., Yao, K., Cai, Z.H., 2017. Research progress in toxicity of carbon quantum dots. Sci. Sin. 47(10), 1170-1178. doi:10.1360/N032017-00038.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhu et al., 2013

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhu et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Waste utilization of synthetic carbon quantum dots based on tea and peanut shell
